topic: Properties and Functions of the Cell

subUnits.title: 6.1 Feeding and Nutrition in Cells
notes: Feeding in Cells:
Definition:
Feeding (nutrition) is the process by which organisms obtain and utilize food substances for growth, energy, and maintenance of life processes.
Types of Nutrition:
1. Autotrophic Nutrition:
• Definition: Organisms make their own food from simple inorganic substances
• Types:
a) Photosynthetic Nutrition:
- Using light energy to synthesize food
- Occurs in green plants, algae, and some bacteria
- Equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
- Nigerian example: Cassava, maize, and yam plants producing food via photosynthesis
b) Chemosynthetic Nutrition:
- Using chemical energy from oxidation of inorganic compounds
- Occurs in certain bacteria (nitrifying, sulfur bacteria)
- Example: Nitrifying bacteria converting ammonia to nitrates
- Nigerian example: Soil bacteria fixing nitrogen for plant growth in Nigerian farms
2. Heterotrophic Nutrition:
• Definition: Organisms obtain food by consuming other organisms or organic matter
• Types:
a) Holozoic Nutrition:
- Ingestion of solid food followed by digestion and absorption
- Occurs in animals and humans
- Process: Ingestion → Digestion → Absorption → Assimilation → Egestion
- Nigerian example: Humans eating varied diets of rice, beans, vegetables
b) Saprophytic Nutrition:
- Feeding on dead organic matter
- Secrete enzymes externally, absorb digested nutrients
- Examples: Fungi (mushrooms, molds), some bacteria
- Nigerian example: Fungi decomposing organic waste; yeast in local brewing
c) Parasitic Nutrition:
- Feeding on living hosts, causing harm
- Derive nutrients from host organism
- Examples: Tapeworms, Plasmodium (malaria parasite), mistletoe
- Nigerian example: Plasmodium causing malaria; tapeworms in livestock
d) Symbiotic Nutrition:
- Mutualistic relationship where both organisms benefit
- Examples: Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules
- Nigerian example: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria improving soil fertility for crops
Mode of Nutrition in Different Organisms:
1. In Plants:
• Primarily autotrophic (photosynthetic)
• Some plants are parasitic (mistletoe, dodder)
• Some are saprophytic (Indian pipe)
• Nigerian context: Most Nigerian crops are autotrophic
2. In Animals:
• All animals are heterotrophic
• Different feeding mechanisms:
- Filter feeding: Straining particles from water (fish)
- Fluid feeding: Sucking liquid food (mosquitoes, aphids)
- Chewing/crushing: Biting and grinding solid food (humans, cattle)
- Swallowing whole: Ingesting prey whole (snakes, frogs)
• Nigerian example: Cattle grazing on grass; humans eating solid meals
3. In Microorganisms:
• Bacteria: Autotrophic (cyanobacteria) or heterotrophic (pathogenic bacteria)
• Fungi: Mostly saprophytic or parasitic
• Protozoa: Mostly holozoic or parasitic
• Nigerian context: Microorganisms in soil, water, and human body
Importance of Understanding Nutrition:
1. For Health and Medicine:
• Understanding nutrient requirements prevents malnutrition
• Basis for dietary recommendations and disease prevention
• Nigerian example: Addressing protein-energy malnutrition in children
2. For Agriculture:
• Knowledge of plant nutrition improves crop yields
• Understanding animal nutrition enhances livestock production
• Nigerian example: Fertilizer application based on soil nutrient needs
3. For Environmental Management:
• Understanding nutrient cycles helps manage ecosystems
• Prevents pollution from excess nutrients (eutrophication)
• Nigerian example: Managing fertilizer runoff into water bodies
4. For Biotechnology:
• Developing nutrient-enriched crops (biofortification)
• Improving food preservation and processing
• Nigerian example: Developing vitamin-A enriched cassava varieties
Nutrient Requirements:
• Macronutrients: Required in large amounts
- Carbohydrates: Energy source (rice, yam, cassava)
- Proteins: Growth and repair (beans, fish, meat)
- Fats: Energy storage and insulation (palm oil, groundnuts)
• Micronutrients: Required in small amounts
- Vitamins: Regulate body processes (fruits, vegetables)
- Minerals: Structural and regulatory functions (salt, dairy)
- Water: Medium for biochemical reactions
• Nigerian context: Balanced diet combining local foods ensures adequate nutrition
Practical Activities:
1. Testing Food Samples:
• Teacher sets up experiment to show the effects of different nutrients on Spirogyra
• Students observe, record, and discuss experiments
• Test for starch using iodine solution
• Test for protein using biuret reagent
• Test for fats using ethanol emulsion test
2. Observing Feeding Mechanisms:
• Observe insects feeding on plants
• Observe fish filter feeding in aquarium
• Nigerian context: Observing local animals and their feeding habits
3. Growing Plants Under Different Conditions:
• Grow plants with and without light
• Observe effects on growth and color
• Nigerian context: Understanding importance of sunlight for crop growth
Common Misconceptions:
• 'All autotrophs are plants': Some bacteria and protists are also autotrophic
• 'Parasites are always harmful': Some parasitic relationships can be beneficial in ecosystems
• 'Nutrition is only about eating': Nutrition includes all processes from food acquisition to utilization
• 'More food always means better nutrition': Quality and balance matter more than quantity
Conclusion:
• Nutrition is fundamental to all life processes and ecosystem functioning
• Different organisms have evolved diverse strategies to obtain and utilize nutrients
• Understanding nutrition has practical applications in health, agriculture, and environment
• In Nigeria, nutritional knowledge supports food security, public health, and sustainable development

opts: Making food from simple inorganic substances
quiz.explanation: Autotrophs synthesize their own food from inorganic substances using light or chemical energy.
Leaf mesophyll cells contain chloroplasts where photosynthesis takes place.
Saprophytes like fungi obtain nutrients by decomposing dead organic matter.

quiz.q: A parasitic organism in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Plasmodium is a parasitic protozoan that causes malaria by feeding on human blood cells.
Filter feeders have specialized structures to strain small food particles from water.

quiz.q: A Nigerian example of symbiotic nutrition is:
opts: Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules
quiz.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen for legumes while receiving carbohydrates, a mutualistic relationship.
Macronutrients are required in large amounts and include carbohydrates, proteins, and fats for energy and growth.

quiz.q: Understanding nutrition is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Guides fertilizer application and crop improvement
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of plant nutrition helps farmers optimize soil fertility and crop yields through appropriate nutrient management.

quiz.q: A common misconception about nutrition is:
opts: More food always means better nutrition
Different organisms have different nutritional needs
quiz.explanation: Nutritional quality and balance matter more than quantity; excessive intake of some nutrients can be harmful.
Chemosynthetic bacteria derive energy from chemical reactions involving inorganic compounds like ammonia or sulfur.
